platform.name: React Native
platform.organization: Meta Platforms, Inc. and the React Native community

category: Frameworks, mobile, games, hardware, and creative ecosystems

surface.name: Recommended React Native framework path: Expo tutorial, Create your first app
surface.selection_basis: The current React Native Get Started page says most new-app developers benefit from a framework, recommends the Expo framework, gives create-expo-app, and directs the developer to Expo's getting-started guide. The linked official Expo tutorial has an explicitly bounded Create your first app chapter that creates, runs, edits, and visibly verifies a React Native app.

alternatives_considered.name: React Native Introduction interactive Snack
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It provides an even shorter browser-only edit, but the page says getting-started instructions have moved to Environment setup; the Environment setup page recommends the framework path for new apps.

alternatives_considered.name: Get Started Without a Framework
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: React Native documents this for developers whose constraints are not served by a framework or who prefer to write their own, rather than as the recommended new-app route.

alternatives_considered.name: Expo Get started sequence
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This shorter product setup sequence creates and runs a generic Expo project; the official learning tutorial provides a named first-app chapter with a visible code-change success boundary.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create the recommended framework-based React Native app, run it on a physical device and web, make the tutorial's first visible code change, and observe the update.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no React Native or Expo project.
Node.js, a code editor, Expo Go, and TypeScript or React familiarity are not assumed.
No Expo account, paid EAS plan, native app-store account, Xcode, or Android Studio is required by this tutorial path.

documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Create the StickerSmash Expo and React Native project, reset it to the tutorial baseline, run it on mobile and web, edit app/index.tsx with React Native core components and styles, and see the saved change applied to the running apps.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The connected mobile and web apps show a dark #25292e screen with centered white Home screen text, followed by the chapter summary stating that the developer successfully created a new Expo project and used React Native core components.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The linked Expo chapter is titled Create your first app and ends after the saved code is applied to the running apps, with a success summary before the next chapter.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: React Native explicitly delegates its recommended new-project route to Expo. The first linked tutorial chapter names the milestone, supplies a visible running-app verification, summarizes completion, and separates later navigation work into the next chapter.

prerequisites.requirement: A macOS, Linux, or Windows developer machine with a terminal, plus a physical Android or iOS device for the tutorial's mobile path.
Node.js LTS installed on the developer machine.
Expo Go installed on the physical Android or iOS device.
VS Code or another preferred code editor or IDE.

Network access to download npm packages and tutorial assets and to connect the development server to Expo Go; the physical-device route normally requires the phone and computer to communicate over the same network.

primary_path.action: Open React Native's current Get Started page.
details: Review the framework recommendation and the distinction between framework and no-framework paths.
primary_path.input: Intent to start a new React Native application.
primary_path.success_signal: Start a new React Native project with Expo is visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Developers with constraints not served by a framework must take the documented no-framework branch.

primary_path.action: Follow Continue with Expo to the official Expo getting-started material and open Create your first app.
details: The React Native page provides create-expo-app and delegates the rest of the recommended path to Expo.
primary_path.success_signal: The chapter title, prerequisites, and five numbered sections are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Install or verify Node.js LTS.
details: create-expo-app and the Expo development server run through Node.js tooling.
primary_path.success_signal: npx is available in the terminal.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The tutorial links to Node.js but does not reproduce platform-specific installation steps.

primary_path.action: Install or choose a code editor.
details: The tutorial names VS Code and permits another preferred editor or IDE.
primary_path.output: Editor capable of modifying TypeScript files.
primary_path.success_signal: The project can be opened and app/index.tsx edited.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Editor installation is linked but not decomposed.

primary_path.action: Install Expo Go on a physical Android or iOS device.
details: The environment guide recommends a real device for seeing what users see and describes Expo Go as the quick learner path.
primary_path.input: Compatible physical device and app-store access.
primary_path.success_signal: Expo Go can scan a QR code on Android, or the iOS camera can open the Expo link.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Device, store, organization policy, or network restrictions can prevent installation.

primary_path.action: Run npx create-expo-app@latest StickerSmash.
details: create-expo-app initializes the new React Native project and installs the Expo package, Expo CLI, Expo Router, tab navigation, TypeScript, and multi-platform configuration from the default template.
primary_path.input: Node.js LTS, network, writable working directory.
primary_path.output: Template-selection prompt and then project generation.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI offers an Expo SDK template choice.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Package download, registry, proxy, disk, or filesystem failures can stop initialization.

primary_path.action: Select the SDK 54 template when prompted.
details: The current tutorial explicitly says it is designed for SDK 54 and that Expo Go on a physical device uses that template during the documented SDK transition.
primary_path.success_signal: StickerSmash is generated using the SDK 54 default template.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Selecting a different SDK can make the tutorial and installed Expo Go version incompatible.

primary_path.action: Allow create-expo-app to create StickerSmash and install its dependencies.
details: The default template includes the boilerplate and libraries used by the tutorial.
primary_path.success_signal: Project generation finishes without an error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No completion duration is stated; dependency installation can fail for environment or network reasons.

primary_path.action: Change into the project directory with cd StickerSmash.
details: All remaining commands are run from the generated project.
primary_path.success_signal: Project files are visible from the shell.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A different project name changes the directory name.

primary_path.action: Download the tutorial assets archive.
details: The assets are used throughout the StickerSmash tutorial, including later chapters.
primary_path.success_signal: The archive is available locally.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Browser or filesystem restrictions can prevent download.

primary_path.action: Unzip the asset archive and replace the default files in StickerSmash/assets/images.
details: Preserve the tutorial's expected assets directory.
primary_path.output: Tutorial image assets in the project.
primary_path.success_signal: The replacement files are present under assets/images.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect extraction location or merge behavior leaves the expected assets absent.

primary_path.action: Open the project directory in the chosen editor or IDE.
details: The later app/index.tsx edit is performed here.
primary_path.success_signal: app and assets directories are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Run npm run reset-project.
details: The script removes the default app boilerplate from the live app directory and preserves the prior files in app-example.
primary_path.output: Minimal app directory with index.tsx and _layout.tsx; prior example material moved to app-example.
primary_path.success_signal: Only the two documented app files remain in app and the previous content is in app-example.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Script or dependency failures prevent the documented baseline.

primary_path.action: Start the development server with npx expo start.
details: Keep this terminal process running while testing and editing.
primary_path.output: Expo development server and terminal QR code.
primary_path.success_signal: A QR code appears in the terminal.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Port, dependency, network, or project errors can prevent the server from starting.

primary_path.action: Open the QR-code project on the physical device.
details: On Android, open Expo Go and select Scan QR code; on iOS, scan with the default Camera app.
primary_path.input: Running server, QR code, Expo Go, and device connectivity.
primary_path.output: StickerSmash running on the physical device.
primary_path.success_signal: The initial app screen renders on the device.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the phone cannot connect, confirm the phone and computer are on the same Wi-Fi; the official AI tutorial also documents restarting with npx expo start --tunnel as a fallback.

primary_path.action: Press W in the Expo terminal to open the web app.
details: The tutorial asks the developer to run on mobile and web and shows the app on all platforms.
primary_path.output: Web build opened in the default browser.
primary_path.success_signal: The initial app renders in the browser.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Web compilation or browser-launch errors prevent the second platform from rendering.

primary_path.action: Replace app/index.tsx with the documented React Native component and style code.
details: Import Text, View, and StyleSheet from react-native.
Render a View containing Text with Home screen.
Define the container with flex 1, backgroundColor #25292e, centered alignment and justification, and define white text with #fff.
primary_path.success_signal: The editor shows the documented component and styles without syntax errors.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect import, JSX, or style syntax can prevent compilation.

primary_path.action: Save app/index.tsx.
details: Expo sends and applies the saved change to running apps connected to the development server.
primary_path.output: Development server receives the changed module.
primary_path.success_signal: Connected apps reload with the update.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A compile error is shown instead of the new screen until the code is corrected and saved again.

primary_path.action: Verify the updated screen on the connected mobile device and web app.
details: Compare the result with the tutorial's App with updated text and background color image.
primary_path.input: Saved valid component and running connected apps.
primary_path.output: Visible first edited React Native application.
primary_path.success_signal: Both running targets show centered white Home screen text on a dark #25292e background.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the old screen remains, verify the server connection and saved file; if an error appears, correct the code and save again.

primary_path.action: Reach the chapter summary.
details: The summary states that a new Expo project was created and React Native core components were used, and that the developer is ready to continue.
primary_path.success_signal: Chapter 1 summary appears before Next: Chapter 2 Add navigation.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

branches.condition: A framework does not serve the developer's constraints, or the developer prefers to write a framework.
branches.path: Set up the native environment and follow Get Started Without a Framework, using React Native Community CLI, Metro, Android or iOS native tooling, App.tsx edits, and reload verification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the Expo path with a substantially longer operating-system and native-platform-specific journey.

branches.condition: The developer chooses an emulator, simulator, or production-oriented development build instead of Expo Go on a physical device.
branches.path: Use the corresponding Expo environment option and its Android Studio, Xcode, simulator, emulator, or development-build setup.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds platform-specific native tools and configuration not included in the selected tutorial route.

branches.condition: The developer uses yarn, pnpm, or Bun instead of npm.
branches.path: Select the matching package-manager tabs and use the equivalent creation, reset, and start commands.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes command syntax and package-manager installation requirements without changing the target app.

branches.condition: The physical device cannot connect over the local network.
branches.path: Confirm both devices are on the same Wi-Fi; if still blocked, stop the server with Ctrl+C and restart it with npx expo start --tunnel.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds connection diagnosis and tunnel startup before mobile verification.

branches.condition: The mobile device is Android rather than iOS.
branches.path: Use Expo Go > Scan QR code; on iOS use the default Camera app to scan the terminal QR code.

branches.condition: The developer only needs one target for an initial local proof.
branches.path: Run either the physical-device path or press W for web; the selected tutorial nevertheless asks to run mobile and web and shows all platforms.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Reduces target setup, but does not literally complete the selected chapter's all-platform run instruction.

friction_gates.description: Node.js LTS must be installed before create-expo-app can run.
The chosen physical device must support and permit installing Expo Go.
Package registry, proxy, network, disk, and filesystem conditions must allow project and dependency creation.
The current tutorial requires the SDK 54 template, while current create-expo-app documentation describes an SDK 57 transition and different choices for physical Expo Go versus other targets.
The development machine and physical device need a working connection, normally same-network connectivity, or the tunnel fallback.
The tutorial assumes TypeScript and React familiarity for understanding and correcting the first component edit.

excluded_after_success: Chapter 2 navigation and the remaining StickerSmash tutorial chapters for screen UI, image picker, modal, gestures, screenshots, platform differences, and application appearance.
Expo development builds, EAS builds, distribution, app-store submission, web deployment, updates, and monitoring.
Native-module development, production signing, Android Studio or Xcode setup, testing, debugging, performance, release, and existing-app integration.
The optional deletion of app-example after reset, because the chapter says it can be deleted but does not require it for first success.

uncertainties.question: Should the inline Snack edit count as React Native's first success instead of a local Expo app?
uncertainties.impact: The Snack route needs only a browser and a one-line text edit, eliminating project, package, device, server, and asset setup.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Introduction offers the immediate interactive edit, but explicitly says getting-started instructions moved to Environment setup, whose new-app guidance recommends Expo and delegates to Expo's guide.

uncertainties.question: Will the SDK 54 template prompt remain current throughout the research snapshot's lifetime?
uncertainties.impact: Template choice determines Expo Go compatibility and generated file structure.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current tutorial requires SDK 54 during an explicitly temporary SDK 57 transition, so the step is version-sensitive.

uncertainties.question: Are all mobile and web targets mandatory for the first-app boundary?
uncertainties.impact: Requiring every displayed platform adds device setup and web compilation compared with one successful target.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The chapter says to run on mobile and web and says once running on all platforms, but a developer could observe the first code change on a single connected target.

uncertainties.question: What exact steps are required to install Node.js, the editor, or Expo Go on the developer's chosen systems?
uncertainties.impact: External installation and organization-policy steps can dominate a clean-machine journey.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The selected tutorial states and links these prerequisites but does not reproduce each vendor's installation flow.

sources.title: Get Started with React Native
sections_used: Start a new React Native project with Expo
evidence_supported: Framework recommendation, Expo selection, create command, no-framework alternative, and delegation to Expo

sources.title: Create your first app
sections_used: Run the app on mobile and web
evidence_supported: Full selected project, asset, reset, run, edit, live-apply, and chapter-completion path

sources.title: Set up your environment
sections_used: Where would you like to develop?
How would you like to develop?
Set up an Android device with Expo Go
evidence_supported: Real-device recommendation, Expo Go learner route, development-build alternative, and Android installation

sources.title: Create your first app, Build with AI tutorial
sections_used: Run the app on your phone
evidence_supported: Current SDK 54 physical-device condition, same-Wi-Fi requirement, tunnel recovery, and visible edit result

sources.title: React Native Introduction
evidence_supported: JavaScript prerequisite and immediate browser Snack alternative
